html:
[5049]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 虎恤人 - Phrase meaning 'the tiger spares the man,' indicating mercy or restraint.
  • 頻呼大王 - Means 'frequently calling out to the Great King,' a term of address used by the man to the tiger.
  • 弭耳如喜狀 - Describes the tiger lowering its ears in a manner suggesting happiness or contentment.
  • 須防負李行 - Means 'the tiger carries the man on its back and walks,' with '須防' implying a cautious or protective action.
  • 投一窟中 - Means 'to a cave,' indicating the tiger's destination.
  • 二三子見人喜躍 - Means 'the cubs see the man and are delighted,' showing the cubs' reaction to the man's presence.
  • 虎于窟上俯视久之方去 - Means 'the tiger looks down from the cave for a long time before leaving,' describing the tiger's behavior after dropping the man.
  • 其后入窟恒分所得之肉 - Means 'later, when entering the cave, the tiger always shares the meat it has caught,' indicating the tiger's habit of sharing food.
  • 及李积十余日子大如犬 - Means 'and the man's sons grow up, and by the time they are over ten days old, they are as big as dogs,' describing the growth of the man's offspring.
  • 悉能陆梁 - Means 'they can all jump and run,' indicating the cubs' abilities.
  • 乳虎因负出窟 - Means 'the mother tiger carries them out of the cave,' describing the cubs' first outing.
  • 至第三子 - Means 'to the third cub,' indicating the tiger's final visit.
  • 李恐去尽则已死窟中 - Means 'the man fears that if the tiger leaves, he will die in the cave,' expressing the man's concern.
  • 乃因抱之云大王独不相引 - Means 'so he hugs the tiger and says, 'Great King, why don't you take me with you?'' showing the man's plea.
  • 虎因垂尾 - Means 'the tiger lowers its tail,' a sign of submission or agreement.
  • 李持之遂得出窟 - Means 'the man holds onto the tiger's tail and is taken out of the cave,' describing the man's escape.
  • 李复云幸已相祐 - Means 'the man then says, 'Thank you for saving me,' expressing gratitude.
  • 岂不送至某家 - Means 'Won't you take me to my home?'' showing the man's request.
  • 虎又负李至所取处而诀 - Means 'the tiger carries the man to his home and then leaves,' describing the tiger's final act.
  • 毎三日一至李舍如相防 - Means 'every three days, the tiger comes to the man's house as if to visit,' indicating the tiger's regular visits.
  • 经二十日前后五六度 - Means 'over twenty days, this happened five or six times,' describing the frequency of the visits.
  • 村人怕惧 - Means 'the villagers are afraid,' showing their reaction to the tiger's visits.
  • 愿勿来 - Means 'I wish you wouldn't come anymore,' expressing the man's plea to the tiger.
  • 经月余复一来自尔乃绝焉 - Means 'after a month or so, the tiger comes once more and then stops coming,' indicating the end of the tiger's visits.
  • 出广异录 - Means 'from the *Guangyi Lu* (Records of Strange Things),' indicating the source of the story.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鳯翔府 - Refers to Fengxiang Prefecture, a historical administrative region in Shaanxi Province.
  • 李将军 - Refers to a general named Li, indicating a military figure.
  • 虎 - Refers to a tiger, a symbol of power and ferocity in Chinese culture.
  • 大王 - A title of respect, often used to address powerful figures or animals.
  • 窟 - Refers to a cave, a common setting in folktales and legends.
  • 二三子 - Refers to the tiger's cubs, indicating the tiger's offspring.
  • 乳虎 - Refers to the mother tiger, emphasizing her role in caring for the cubs.
  • 村人 - Refers to the villagers, indicating the local community.

Proper Nouns

  • 鳯翔府 - Fengxiang Prefecture.
  • 李将军 - General Li.
  • 虎 - Tiger.
  • 大王 - Great King.
  • 窟 - Cave.
  • 二三子 - Cubs.
  • 乳虎 - Mother Tiger.
  • 村人 - Villagers.
  • 广异录 - *Guangyi Lu* (Records of Strange Things).

[5050]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 化为虎 - Literal meaning 'transformed into a tiger'; used to describe a supernatural transformation.
  • 恒引外虎入村 - Literal meaning 'constantly luring external tigers into the village'; suggests a deliberate action.
  • 盗食牛畜 - Literal meaning 'stealing and eating cattle and livestock'; indicates destructive behavior.
  • 相恶之辞 - Literal meaning 'words of mutual hatred'; used to describe accusations of malice.
  • 见人肥充者便欲噉之 - Literal meaning 'seeing people who are well-fed, he wants to eat them'; describes the predatory nature of the transformed individual.
  • 察其举措如醉也 - Literal meaning 'observing his actions as if he were drunk'; suggests a state of confusion or intoxication.
  • 以理喻遣之 - Literal meaning 'to persuade him with reason'; indicates an attempt to reason with the individual.
  • 号哭 - Literal meaning 'to cry out in sorrow'; indicates a strong emotional response.
  • 俛仰有似悲怆 - Literal meaning 'looking up and down as if in sorrow'; describes a mournful expression.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 涪陵里正范端 - Refers to Fan Duan, the village head of Fuoling, who is the main character of the story.
  • 州县任使 - Refers to serving in state and county offices, indicating a position of authority.
  • 耆老 - Refers to elderly village leaders or elders.
  • 县令 - Refers to the local magistrate or county official.
  • 广异记 - Refers to the *Guangyi Ji* (Records of Strange Events), a collection of supernatural tales.

Proper Nouns

  • 范端 - The name of the village head who transforms into a tiger.
  • 涪陵 - The name of the village where the story takes place.
  • 广异记 - The title of the collection of supernatural tales in which this story is found.

[5051]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初为里正不之好也 - At first he was a village headman, but he did not like it or approve of it.
  • 遂服儒号书生 - He then adopted the attire of a scholar and called himself a student of Confucianism.
  • 因向郭买衣 - Because he went to the market or city to buy clothes.
  • 我明日日中得书生石井崖充食 - I will at noon tomorrow obtain the scholar Shi Jingya to fill my hunger.
  • 可令其除去刀杖勿有损伤 - You can have him remove his weapons and avoid any harm.
  • 石井崖见道士道士不见石井崖 - Shi Jingya saw the Taoist priest, but the Taoist priest did not see Shi Jingya.
  • 井崖闻此言惊骇 - Shi Jingya heard these words and was greatly alarmed.
  • 行至店宿留连数宿 - He walked to an inn and stayed there for several nights.
  • 忽有军人来问井崖莫要擕军器去否 - Suddenly a soldier came and asked Shi Jingya if he wanted to take his military equipment with him.
  • 井崖素闻道士言乃出刀防鎗头懐中藏之 - Shi Jingya, having heard the Taoist priest's words, took out his sword and gun and hid them in his bosom.
  • 军人刀去 - The soldier took the sword and left.
  • 井崖盘桓未行 - Shi Jingya lingered and did not leave.
  • 店主屡逐之 - The innkeeper repeatedly chased him away.
  • 井崖不得已遂以竹盛却鎗头而行 - Shi Jingya had no choice but to put the gun head in a bamboo tube and continue on his way.
  • 至路口见一虎当路径前攫取井崖 - He came to a road intersection and saw a tiger blocking the path in front of him, trying to grab him.
  • 井崖遂以鎗刺中其心遂毙 - Shi Jingya then used his gun to stab the tiger, hitting its heart and killing it.
  • 二童子审观虎死乃讴謌喜跃 - The two boys examined the dead tiger and then sang and leaped with joy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 石井崖 - A specific place name, likely a cliff or rock formation with a well or spring.
  • 里正 - A village headman or local official.
  • 服儒号书生 - Adopting the attire of a scholar and calling oneself a student of Confucianism.
  • 道士 - A Taoist priest or hermit.
  • 朱衣 - A red robe, often associated with Taoist priests.
  • 青衣童子 - Green-robed boys, often depicted as attendants to Taoist priests.
  • 书生 - A scholar or student of Confucianism.
  • 刀杖 - Swords and spears, military equipment.
  • 军人 - Soldiers.
  • 竹盛却鎗头 - Putting the gun head in a bamboo tube.
  • 虎 - Tiger, a common symbol in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 讴謌喜跃 - Singing and leaping with joy.

Proper Nouns

  • 石井崖 - A specific place name, likely a cliff or rock formation with a well or spring.
  • 里正 - A village headman or local official.
  • 服儒号书生 - Adopting the attire of a scholar and calling oneself a student of Confucianism.
  • 道士 - A Taoist priest or hermit.
  • 朱衣 - A red robe, often associated with Taoist priests.
  • 青衣童子 - Green-robed boys, often depicted as attendants to Taoist priests.
  • 书生 - A scholar or student of Confucianism.
  • 刀杖 - Swords and spears, military equipment.
  • 军人 - Soldiers.
  • 竹盛却鎗头 - Putting the gun head in a bamboo tube.
  • 虎 - Tiger, a common symbol in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 讴謌喜跃 - Singing and leaping with joy.

[5052]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 械虎 - Metaphor for dealing with a powerful or dangerous adversary.
  • 襄梁间 - Region between Xiang and Liang, two ancient states in China.
  • 兽 - Rare or mythical beast, possibly a typo or variant of a known creature.
  • 槛穽 - Traps or snares used for capturing animals.
  • 移厨命賔寮 - Formal command to move the kitchen and summon guests, indicating a ceremonial or official event.
  • 挍往临之 - To go and inspect or oversee the situation.
  • 设幄幙 - To set up tents or pavilions, often used in ceremonial or festive contexts.
  • 防之 - To guard or protect, implying a sense of vigilance or caution.
  • 徐徐以土填之 - To slowly fill the trap with soil, indicating a deliberate and cautious action.
  • 迤逦合其荷板 - To gradually close the trap's lid, suggesting a methodical approach.
  • 蹙而钉之 - To press and nail the trap shut, emphasizing the finality of the action.
  • 四面以索趂之而行 - To tie the tiger with ropes and lead it away, indicating control and restraint.
  • 防者随而笑之 - The guards or onlookers laugh at the situation, suggesting a sense of triumph or amusement.
  • 势穷力竭而取之 - To capture the tiger when it is exhausted and powerless, emphasizing the importance of timing and strategy.
  • 牵羊拽犬 - Metaphor for easily handling weak or submissive creatures.
  • 纤牙利爪 - Sharp teeth and claws, symbolizing the potential danger of the tiger.
  • 欲制强敌者 - Those who wish to control or defeat a powerful enemy.
  • 当如是乎 - Should they act in this manner?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 襄梁间 - Region between Xiang and Liang, two ancient states in China, indicating the historical and geographical context.
  • 兽 - Rare or mythical beast, possibly a typo or variant of a known creature, suggesting the exotic or supernatural elements in the story.
  • 槛穽 - Traditional Chinese traps used for capturing animals, reflecting the practical and cultural practices of the time.
  • 移厨命賔寮 - Formal command in ancient Chinese society, indicating the hierarchical and ceremonial nature of the event.
  • 设幄幙 - Setting up tents or pavilions, often used in ceremonial or festive contexts, reflecting the social and cultural practices of the time.
  • 防之 - Guarding or protecting, implying a sense of vigilance or caution, reflecting the societal values of the time.
  • 徐徐以土填之 - Slowly filling the trap with soil, indicating a deliberate and cautious action, reflecting the practical and methodical approach of the time.
  • 迤逦合其荷板 - Gradually closing the trap's lid, suggesting a methodical approach, reflecting the practical and methodical approach of the time.
  • 蹙而钉之 - Pressing and nailing the trap shut, emphasizing the finality of the action, reflecting the practical and methodical approach of the time.
  • 四面以索趂之而行 - Tying the tiger with ropes and leading it away, indicating control and restraint, reflecting the practical and methodical approach of the time.
  • 防者随而笑之 - The guards or onlookers laughing at the situation, suggesting a sense of triumph or amusement, reflecting the social and cultural practices of the time.
  • 势穷力竭而取之 - Capturing the tiger when it is exhausted and powerless, emphasizing the importance of timing and strategy, reflecting the practical and methodical approach of the time.
  • 牵羊拽犬 - Metaphor for easily handling weak or submissive creatures, reflecting the cultural and metaphorical language of the time.
  • 纤牙利爪 - Sharp teeth and claws, symbolizing the potential danger of the tiger, reflecting the cultural and metaphorical language of the time.
  • 欲制强敌者 - Those who wish to control or defeat a powerful enemy, reflecting the strategic and military context of the time.
  • 当如是乎 - Should they act in this manner? Reflecting the rhetorical and philosophical questions of the time.

Proper Nouns

  • 襄梁间 - The region between Xiang and Liang.
  • 兽 - A rare or mythical beast.
  • 槛穽 - Traps or snares.
  • 移厨命賔寮 - A formal command to move the kitchen and summon guests.
  • 挍往临之 - To go and inspect or oversee the situation.
  • 设幄幙 - To set up tents or pavilions.
  • 防之 - To guard or protect.
  • 徐徐以土填之 - To slowly fill the trap with soil.
  • 迤逦合其荷板 - To gradually close the trap's lid.
  • 蹙而钉之 - To press and nail the trap shut.
  • 四面以索趂之而行 - To tie the tiger with ropes and lead it away.
  • 防者随而笑之 - The guards or onlookers laugh at the situation.
  • 势穷力竭而取之 - To capture the tiger when it is exhausted and powerless.
  • 牵羊拽犬 - A metaphor for easily handling weak or submissive creatures.
  • 纤牙利爪 - Sharp teeth and claws.
  • 欲制强敌者 - Those who wish to control or defeat a powerful enemy.
  • 当如是乎 - Should they act in this manner?

[5053]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 兽 - A rare or mythical beast, possibly a variant of 'fierce beast'.
  • 行旅 - Refers to travelers or journeyers.
  • 攫 - Means to seize or snatch, often used in the context of predatory animals.
  • 藂薄 - Refers to dense underbrush or thicket.
  • 岩崖 - Means cliff or precipice.
  • 清溪 - Refers to a clear stream or creek.
  • 洞口 - Means the entrance of a cave.
  • 小虎子 - Refers to young tigers or cubs.
  • 顾望 - Means to look back or gaze.
  • 忻忻然 - Describes a state of happiness or contentment.
  • 置 - Means to place or set.
  • 堕 - Means to fall or drop.
  • 生致 - Means to capture alive or bring to life.
  • 危哉 - An exclamation meaning 'dangerous indeed' or 'how perilous'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 商山路 - A specific road or path, possibly named after the region of Shangshan, which may have historical or cultural significance.
  • 虎 - The tiger is a significant symbol in Chinese culture, often representing power, danger, and ferocity.
  • 演诸子 - Refers to the teachings or principles of various philosophical schools, particularly the Hundred Schools of Thought.
  • 玉堂闲话 - A collection of anecdotes or tales, likely from a classical text or anthology.

Proper Nouns

  • 商山路 - A specific road or path.
  • 玉堂闲话 - A collection of anecdotes or tales, likely from a classical text or anthology.

[5054]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 清源人陈褒 - Refers to a person named Chen Biao from Qingyuan.
  • 别业 - Means a secondary residence or vacation home.
  • 临牕夜坐 - Literally 'sitting by the window at night,' describing a specific action.
  • 牕外即旷野 - Means 'outside the window is an open field.'
  • 忽闻有人马声 - Means 'suddenly heard the sound of people and horses.'
  • 视之见一妇人骑虎自牕下过 - Means 'looked and saw a woman riding a tiger pass under the window.'
  • 径入西屋内壁下 - Means 'directly entered the west room's inner wall.'
  • 先有一婢卧 - Means 'there was already a maid sleeping there.'
  • 即取细竹枝从壁隙中刺之 - Means 'immediately took a thin bamboo branch and pierced her through the wall gap.'
  • 婢忽尔腹痛开户如厠 - Means 'the maid suddenly had abdominal pain and opened the door to go to the toilet.'
  • 褒方愕骇未及言 - Means 'Chen Biao was just shocked and didn't have time to speak.'
  • 仅免 - Means 'barely escaped.'
  • 乡人云村中恒有此怪 - Means 'the villagers said there was always such a strange phenomenon in the village.'
  • 所谓虎者也 - Means 'it is called a tiger ghost.'
  • 食虎 - Means 'eating a tiger.'
  • 建安人山中种粟者皆搆棚于髙树以防虎 - Means 'those who planted millet in the mountains of Jian'an all built shelters on high trees to prevent tigers.'
  • 尝有一人方升棚 - Means 'once there was a person climbing the shelter.'
  • 见一虎垂头塌耳过去甚速 - Means 'saw a tiger with its head down and ears drooping pass very quickly.'
  • 俄有一兽如虎而稍小蹑前虎而去 - Means 'soon a creature like a tiger but slightly smaller followed the tiger away.'
  • 遂闻竹林中哮吼震地久之乃息 - Means 'then heard roaring in the bamboo forest that shook the ground, and it eventually stopped.'
  • 明日往视其虎遇食略尽但存少骨尔 - Means 'the next day, when they went to look at the tiger, they found it had been eaten almost entirely, leaving only a few bones.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 清源 - A place name, likely referring to a specific region or town.
  • 建安 - A place name, likely referring to a specific region or town.
  • 虎 - Refers to a supernatural entity, a tiger ghost, which is a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 食虎 - The idea of a creature eating a tiger is unusual and adds to the supernatural element of the story.

Proper Nouns

  • 陈褒 - The name of the person in the story.
  • 虎 - The name given to the supernatural entity.

[5055]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 号税人场 - Likely means 'named Tax People’s Field' or 'called the Tax People’s Field,' referring to a place where taxes were collected or where people were taxed, possibly also a place frequented by bandits or dangerous animals.
  • 结伴而行 - 'To travel in company' or 'to go together in groups.'
  • 带甲列队而过 - 'To pass in armor, in formation' – a military term.
  • 行旅赖之 - 'Travelers relied on him' – a common phrase indicating gratitude or dependence on someone.
  • 长语以赏之 - 'Wrote a long commentary to praise him' – '长语' could be a title or a literary form.
  • 补军职以壮之 - 'Granted a military position to honor him.'
  • 须得一人代之 - 'Must have one person to replace them' – refers to the belief that a spirit of a person killed by a tiger or drowned must have a human substitute.
  • 虽闻泛言往往而有 - 'Even though it is generally spoken of, it often occurs' – a saying that such phenomena are common.
  • 任土贡输梗于前 - 'Tribute from the land is obstructed in front' – meaning that the collection of tributes is blocked or hindered.
  • 迈西川奏章多取巫峡人虫作 - The character '' is obscure and may be a corruption or misprint. It may be a variant of '譶' (to speak) or '譶言' (words of prophecy or divination). Possibly, 'The Western Shu (Sichuan) often used people and insects from Wuxia (Wu Gorge) to make prophecies or omens.'
  • 得非系国家之盛衰乎 - 'Is this not related to the rise and fall of the state?' – a rhetorical question.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 周雄 - A historical or legendary figure from the late Tang or early Five Dynasties period, known for his bravery in fighting tigers in Sichuan.
  • 唐大顺景福 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Xizong of Tang, with 'Dashiun' (大顺) and 'Jingfu' (景福) being the years of his rule.
  • 西川 - A region in Sichuan during the Tang and Five Dynasties periods.
  • 鱼全諲特进 - A person of high rank, possibly a military or administrative official.
  • 汉源驿 - A驿站 (post station) in Han Yuan, likely a place on the route to Sichuan.
  • 太平广记 - A famous ancient Chinese collection of stories and anecdotes from the Song Dynasty, compiled by Li Fang and others.
  • 钦定四库全书 - The 'Four Treasuries' compiled under the Qianlong Emperor of the Qing Dynasty, indicating that this text was included in this monumental collection.

Proper Nouns

  • 周雄 - A historical or legendary figure, to be kept as is.
  • 唐大顺景福 - Tang dynasty years, to be translated as 'the Dashiun and Jingfu years of the Tang dynasty.'
  • 西川 - 'Western Shu' or 'Western Sichuan,' a historical region.
  • 鱼全諲特进 - A name and title; 'Tiejing' is a high honorific title, possibly 'Grand Counselor' or 'High Official.'
  • 汉源驿 - A驿站 (post station), to be translated as 'Hanyuan Post Station.'
  • 太平广记 - A book title, to be kept in Chinese with a translation note.
  • 钦定四库全书 - The 'Four Treasuries,' a Qing dynasty compilation, to be kept in Chinese with a translation note.
  • 张俊 - A name of a figure in the same volume, to be kept in Chinese with a translation note.
  • 浔阳猎人 - A name of a figure in the same volume, to be kept in Chinese with a translation note.
  • 柳并 - A name of a figure in the same volume, to be kept in Chinese with a translation note.

[5056]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 僧虎 - Likely refers to a tiger associated with a Buddhist monk or a story involving a monk and a tiger.
  • 王瑶 - A person's name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 刘牧 - Another person's name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 姨虎 - Literally 'aunt tiger,' likely a metaphorical or symbolic term, possibly referring to a female tiger or a tiger associated with a family member.
  • 崔韬 - A person's name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 王行言 - Another person's name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 张俊 - A person's name, likely a central character in the story.
  • 宣州溧水县尉元澹 - A title and name, indicating a local official in the region of Xuanzhou, Liushui County.
  • 只承至官 - Likely means 'to serve in an official capacity' or 'to be entrusted with an official position.'
  • 官满却归 - Means 'after completing the term of office, return home.'
  • 冲夜 - Means 'during the night' or 'in the middle of the night.'
  • 誓欲报讐 - Means 'swore to seek revenge.'
  • 奉上 - Means 'to present' or 'to offer.'
  • 傥生归 - Means 'if I return alive.'
  • 酬哺养之恩 - Means 'to repay the kindness of being raised.'
  • 仆贱 - Means 'a lowly servant.'
  • 终身 - Means 'for life.'
  • 固止之 - Means 'firmly stopped him.'
  • 不可 - Means 'cannot be stopped.'
  • 挟两矢 - Means 'holding two arrows.'
  • 携腰斧 - Means 'carrying an axe on the waist.'
  • 下道 - Means 'on the road.'
  • 依近山谷 - Means 'near a valley.'
  • 大树百余株 - Means 'more than a hundred large trees.'
  • 疑近虎穴 - Means 'suspected to be near a tiger's den.'
  • 上树伺之 - Means 'climbed a tree to watch.'
  • 渐明 - Means 'gradually becoming light.'
  • 蹲伏起动 - Means 'crouching and moving.'
  • 虎穴 - Means 'tiger's den.'
  • 裸而复僵 - Means 'naked and then stiff.'
  • 窟中 - Means 'in the den.'
  • 四子 - Means 'four cubs.'
  • 大如狸 - Means 'as big as a weasel.'
  • 掉尾欢跃 - Means 'wagging their tails and jumping happily.'
  • 以舌防死人 - Means 'licking the dead body.'
  • 竞来争食 - Means 'rushing to fight over the food.'
  • 发一箭 - Means 'shot an arrow.'
  • 中虎额 - Means 'hit the tiger's forehead.'
  • 中其脇 - Means 'hit its side.'
  • 傅毒 - Means 'coated with poison.'
  • 惊跃狂乱 - Means 'jumped around wildly in panic.'
  • 顷刻而死 - Means 'died in a moment.'
  • 下树 - Means 'climbed down the tree.'
  • 截虎头 - Means 'cut off the tiger's head.'
  • 杀四子 - Means 'killed the four cubs.'
  • 首蔓贯之 - Means 'headed and strung them up.'
  • 负妻尸 - Means 'carried his wife's body.'
  • 日晓追及澹 - Means 'in the morning, caught up with Yuan Dan.'

  • 求之不得忽见山穴开中有钱焉不知其数 - Means 'He searched but could not find it, then suddenly saw a mountain cave open with money inside, the amount unknown.'
  • 田父入穴十千而归到家又往取之迷不知道逢一人谓曰汝所失牛其直几耶 - Means 'The peasant entered the cave, took ten thousand coins, and returned home. On his way back, he got lost and met a man who asked, 'How much was your lost cow worth?'
  • 田父曰十千人曰汝牛为山神所已付汝牛价何为妄寻言毕不知所在田父乃悟遂归焉 - Means 'The peasant said ten thousand coins. The man said, 'Your cow has been taken by the mountain god, and the payment for the cow has already been given. Why do you still search? After speaking, the man disappeared, and the peasant realized this and returned home.'
  • 金牛 - Means 'Golden Ox.'
  • 长沙西南有金牛冈 - Means 'In the southwest of Changsha, there is a Golden Ox Hill.'
  • 汉武帝时有一田父牵赤牛告渔人曰寄渡江渔人云船小岂胜得牛田父曰但相容不重君船于是人牛俱上及半江牛粪于船田父曰以此相赠既渡渔人怒其汚船以桡拨粪弃水欲尽方觉是金讶其神异乃蹑之但见人牛入岭随而掘之莫能及也今掘处犹存 - Means 'During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, a peasant led a red ox and asked a fisherman to ferry him across the river. The fisherman said, 'My boat is small, how can it carry a cow?' The peasant said, 'Just let us in, it won't overload your boat.' So, the man and the cow got on board. When they reached the middle of the river, the cow defecated on the boat. The peasant said, 'Take this as a gift.' After crossing, the fisherman was angry about the mess and used his oar to push the feces into the water. He almost finished when he realized it was gold. He was amazed by the divine and followed them, but only saw the man and the cow entering a mountain. He then dug where they had entered but could not reach them. Today, the digging site still exists.'

  • There was a man who asked me about this horse along the way, where '' is likely a typographical error and should be '遇' (encounter).
  • By night, I arrived at the Western Land of India, and suddenly lost this horse, where '西竺国' (Western Land of India) is a reference to a distant western country.
  • Since losing this horse, I have searched for it tirelessly for ten years, and no one knows where I am looking for it.
  • Last year on this day, I saw a child in the Gobi Desert who said there was a strange horse flying eastward.
  • Knowing that the horse came from the east, I suspect it is now in China, and there must be someone who has it.
  • I will now return to you a hundred gold pieces, and I must have the horse back.
  • I have a strong preference for good horses, and hearing of this horse's uniqueness, I deeply cherish it.
  • So I bowed to my mother and begged her to let me keep it temporarily to enjoy it for a few days.
  • My mother angrily said, 'If you keep this horse, disaster will surely come.' I was also very angry at my mother's extreme words, so I ordered more than ten servants to guard the horse and sent my mother out. Her house indeed caught fire and burned down, destroying all the wealth. I still saw my grandmother enter the house and leap onto the horse, which then vanished.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '月氏有羊大尾稍割以供賔亦稍自补复有大秦国北有羊子生于土中' is complex and requires careful parsing. It describes the Yuezhi people cutting their tails to provide meat for guests and also for self-sustenance, and then mentions a different practice in the Roman Empire where lambs are born from the ground.
  • The sentence '秦人候其欲萌为垣以绕之其脐连地不可以刀截击鼓惊之而絶因跳鸣食草以一二百口为羣' describes the practice of waiting for a lamb to be born from the ground, building a wall around it, and then killing it by drumming, which causes it to jump and die. The lamb then joins a herd of about a hundred or two hundred.
  • The sentence '齐庄公时有里征者讼三年而狱不决公乃使二人具一羊诅于社二子将羊而刺之洒其血羊起触二子殪于盟所' describes a lawsuit that lasted three years without resolution, leading to a ritual where two people brought a sheep to a shrine, sacrificed it, and were killed by the sheep.
  • The sentence '汉齐人梁文好道其家有神祠建室三四间座上施皂帐常在其中积十数年后因祀事帐中忽有人语自呼高山君大能饮食治病有騐文事甚肃积数年得进其帐中神醉文乃乞得奉见顔色谓文曰授手来文纳手得持其颐髯须甚长文渐绕手卒然引之而闻作杀羊声座中惊起助文引之乃公路家羊也失之七八年不知所在杀之乃绝' describes a supernatural encounter where a man named Li Wen enters a shrine and meets a deity named Gao Shan Jun, who is described as having a long beard. The deity eventually reveals itself to be a sheep that had been missing for seven or eight years and was killed.

  • 永平二年干陁羅國所獻 - Refers to the year 509 AD (Yongping 2) when a white elephant was presented as a gift from the country of Gandhāra (干陁羅國).
  • 背設五采屏風七寳坐床 - Describes the elephant's back adorned with a colorful screen and a seat made of seven treasures, indicating its ornate and luxurious appearance.
  • 眞是異物 - Means 'truly a rare and extraordinary creature,' emphasizing the elephant's uniqueness.
  • 常養於乘黃象 - Indicates that the elephant was usually kept with other elephants.
  • 常曾壞屋毀牆走出於外 - Describes incidents where the elephant caused damage by breaking through walls and exiting its enclosure.
  • 逢即防 - Means 'when it encountered obstacles, it would stop,' suggesting the elephant's cautious nature.
  • 遇牆亦倒 - Describes how the elephant would knock down walls when it encountered them.
  • 百姓驚怖奔走交驰 - Means 'the common people were terrified and ran in all directions,' depicting the chaos caused by the elephant's actions.
  • 太后遂徙象于此坊 - Indicates that the empress dowager moved the elephant to this place after these incidents.
  • 阆州莫徭 - Refers to a group of people in Langzhou who lived by gathering firewood and cutting reeds.
  • 常于江边刈芦 - Describes their regular activity of cutting reeds along the riverbank.
  • 有大象奄至卷之上背 - Means 'a large elephant suddenly appeared and wrapped its trunk around the man's back.'
  • 行百余里深入泽中 - Describes the elephant carrying the man over a hundred miles into a marsh.
  • 泽中有老象卧而喘息痛声甚苦 - Describes an old elephant lying in the marsh, breathing heavily and suffering from pain.
  • 至其所下于地 - Means 'the man was put down on the ground.'
  • 老象举足足中有竹丁 - Describes the old elephant lifting its foot, revealing a bamboo splinter embedded in its hoof.
  • 晓其意以腰绳系竹丁 - Means 'the man understood the elephant's intention and tied a rope around the bamboo splinter with his waist.'
  • 为拔出浓血五六升许 - Describes the man removing the bamboo splinter and extracting about five or six liters of blood.
  • 小象复鼻卷青艾欲令塞疮 - Means 'a smaller elephant wrapped some green herbs in its trunk and wanted to use them to stop the bleeding.'
  • 摘艾熟按以次塞之 - Describes the man picking the herbs and pressing them into the wound.
  • 尽艾方满 - Means 'the herbs were used up, and the wound was finally filled.'
  • 久之病象能起东西行立已而复卧 - Describes the old elephant gradually recovering and being able to stand and move.
  • 回顾小象以鼻指山呦呦有声 - Means 'the old elephant looked back at the smaller elephant and pointed to the mountain with its trunk, making a sound.'
  • 小象乃去 - Describes the smaller elephant leaving.
  • 须臾得一牙至病象见牙大吼意若嫌之 - Means 'soon, the smaller elephant returned with a tusk, and the old elephant saw it and roared loudly, as if it did not like it.'
  • 小象持牙去 - Describes the smaller elephant taking the tusk away.
  • 顷之又将大牙 - Means 'soon, the smaller elephant returned with another tusk.'
  • 莫徭呼象为将军 - Describes the man calling the elephant 'general.'
  • 言未食患饥 - Means 'the man said he was hungry.'
  • 其牙酷大载至洪州有商胡求买累 - Describes the tusks being very large and eventually reaching Hongzhou, where a merchant from India tried to buy them.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 后魏 - The Northern Wei dynasty (386-534 AD), a significant period in Chinese history.
  • 洛水桥南道东有白象坊 - A specific location in Luoyang during the Northern Wei dynasty, where a white elephant was housed.
  • 永平二年 - The second year of the Yongping era, which corresponds to 509 AD.
  • 干陁羅國 - The country of Gandhāra, located in modern-day Afghanistan and Pakistan, known for its cultural and religious significance.
  • 五采屏风七寳坐床 - Ornate decorations on the elephant's back, symbolizing luxury and status.
  • 太后 - The empress dowager, a powerful figure in the Northern Wei court.
  • 阆州 - A region in present-day Sichuan province, known for its rich cultural heritage.
  • 莫徭 - A group of people in Langzhou who lived by gathering firewood and cutting reeds, often associated with rural and semi-nomadic lifestyles.

[5166]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自加直至四十万 - Literally 'from adding up to forty thousand,' this phrase likely refers to a gradual increase in value or quantity, possibly in a commercial context.
  • 本买牙者我也 - Translates to 'I am the one who originally wanted to buy the tooth,' clarifying the speaker's claim.
  • 长者参市违公法 - Means 'the elder participated in the market, violating public law,' suggesting a legal or ethical transgression.
  • 遂相殴击 - Means 'they then fought each other,' indicating a physical altercation.
  • 所由白县县以白府府诘其由 - Means 'the officials reported to the county, and the county reported to the prefecture, which then questioned the reason,' detailing the chain of reporting.
  • 胡初不肯以牙为寳 - Means 'the Huns initially refused to acknowledge the tooth as a treasure,' indicating reluctance or disbelief.
  • 牙中有二龙相躩而立 - Translates to 'there are two dragons inside the tooth, dancing and standing,' describing a fantastical or symbolic feature.
  • 本国重此者以为货 - Means 'in our country, those who value this consider it a commodity,' indicating the cultural significance of the tooth.
  • 洪州乃以牙及牙主二胡并进之天后 - Means 'the Prefecture of Hongzhou then presented the tooth and its two owners to Empress Wu,' detailing the final action.
  • 剖牙果得龙简 - Means 'when the tooth was cut open, it revealed a dragon-shaped jade,' describing the discovery of a hidden treasure.
  • 赐敕阆州每年给五十千尽而复取以终其身 - Means 'the Empress granted an edict to the Prefecture of Langzhou to provide fifty thousand coins annually, to be replenished until the end of their lives,' detailing the reward.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 胡 - Refers to the Huns, a nomadic people historically known for their interactions with Chinese dynasties.
  • 天后 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, who ruled during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 洪州 - The name of an ancient Chinese prefecture, likely referring to the modern-day city of Nanchang in Jiangxi Province.
  • 阆州 - Another ancient Chinese prefecture, likely referring to the modern-day city of Langzhong in Sichuan Province.
  • 龙简 - A dragon-shaped jade, often associated with imperial symbols and treasures in Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 胡 - The Huns.
  • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian.
  • 洪州 - The Prefecture of Hongzhou.
  • 阆州 - The Prefecture of Langzhou.
  • 龙简 - Dragon-shaped jade.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 象入庄家中庭卧其足下 - Describes an elephant entering a courtyard and lying down near someone's feet.

  • 安南猎者 - '安南' refers to the region of Annam (present-day Vietnam), and '猎者' means a hunter.

  • 每药附箭镞射鸟兽中者必毙 - '每药附箭镞' means to always attach poison to the arrowheads, and '中者必毙' means that anything hit by the poisoned arrow will die.

  • 忽有物触之惊起 - '忽有物触之' means that something suddenly touched him, and '惊起' means he was startled awake.
  • 见是白象大倍他象 - '见是白象' means he saw a white elephant, and '大倍他象' means it was much larger than other elephants.
  • 南人呼之为将军祝之而拜 - '南人' refers to people from the south (Annam), '呼之为将军' means they called it a general, and '祝之而拜' means they prayed to it and bowed before it.
  • 象以鼻卷人上背 - '以鼻卷人上背' means the elephant used its trunk to lift the person onto its back.
  • 复取其弓矢药筒等以授之 - '复取其弓矢药筒等' means it retrieved his bow, arrows, and poison container, and '以授之' means to give them back to him.
  • 因尔遂骋行百余里 - '因尔遂骋行' means thus it galloped, and '百余里' means over a hundred miles.

  • 象至平石战惧且行且望 - '象至平石' means the elephant reached the flat rock, '战惧' means it was trembling with fear, and '且行且望' means it continued walking while looking around.
  • 迳上六十丈象视毕走去 - '迳上六十丈' means the person climbed sixty feet up the tree, '象视毕' means the elephant finished looking, and '走去' means it left.
  • 须臾清朗昨所见大象领凡象百余头循山而来伏于其前 - '须臾清朗' means shortly after, it became clear, '昨所见大象' means the elephant he saw yesterday, '领凡象百余头' means leading a group of about a hundred elephants, '循山而来' means they came along the mountain, and '伏于其前' means they crouched in front of him.
  • 巨兽躩食二象食毕各引去 - '巨兽躩食' means the giant beast ate, '二象食毕' means the two elephants finished eating, and '各引去' means they each left.
  • 人乃思象 - '人乃思象' means the person then thought about the elephant.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 南人 - People from the southern regions, often referring to the area of Annam (Vietnam).

Idiomatic Expressions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 小象乃驰还俄而诸象二百余头来至下皆长跪展转 - The small elephant then gallops back, and soon, over two hundred elephants arrive, all kneeling and turning.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

[5171]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 寳歴中有蒋武者 - During the Baoli period, there was a man named Jiang Wu.

  • 魁梧伟壮 - Tall and robust.
  • 胆气豪勇 - Brave and valiant.
  • 惟求猎射而已 - He only sought hunting and shooting.
  • 善于蹶张 - Skilled in using a crossbow.
  • 每赍弓挟矢 - Whenever he carried his bow and arrows.
  • 剖视其镞皆一一贯心焉 - Upon examination, the arrows were found to have pierced their hearts.
  • 忽有物叩门甚急速 - Suddenly, something knocked on the door very urgently.
  • 隔扉而窥之 - He peered through the door.
  • 见一猩猩跨白象 - He saw a gibbon riding a white elephant.

  • 与象叩吾门何也 - Why did you knock on my door with the elephant?

  • 电光而闪其目劎刃而利其牙 - Its eyes flash like lightning, and its teeth are sharp like swords.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 寳歴 - A period during the Tang Dynasty (618-907).
  • 循州河源 - A place name, Hexian in Xunzhou, a region in southern China.

  • 象 - Elephants, which were not native to China but were known through trade and stories.

  • 蛇目 - Snake's eyes, described as glowing like lightning.

  • 巴蛇 - A mythical creature.
  • 象 - Elephants.
  • 猩猩 - Gibbons.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 黙識 - Literally means 'to secretly recognize' or 'to intuitively understand,' implying a deep, almost subconscious knowledge.
  • 有理者即过 - Means 'those who are right pass by,' suggesting that the elephant allows those who are in the right to proceed unharmed.
  • 以鼻卷之掷空中数丈 - Describes the elephant using its trunk to lift someone and throw them into the air, a vivid and dramatic action.
  • 以牙接之应时碎矣 - Means 'and with its tusks shatters them instantly,' indicating the elephant's swift and decisive action.
  • 莫敢竞者 - Means 'no one dares to dispute,' emphasizing the elephant's authority and the fear it instills.
  • 色黑两牙江猪也 - Describes the elephant as black with two tusks, likening it to a 'river pig,' which may be a metaphor or a colloquial term.
  • 象之所在其土必丰 - Means 'where the elephant is, the land is fertile,' suggesting a connection between the elephant and prosperity.
  • 以水洗牙饮之愈疾 - Describes washing the elephant's tusks with water and drinking it as a cure for diseases.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 出天未晓而其狼自外还入室 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'when the sky was still dark, the wolf returned to the house from outside.'
  • 扄其门 - Means 'locked the door,' where '扄' is an archaic term for locking.
  • 家人甚惧具白于含 - Means 'the family was very frightened and reported it to Han,' where '具白' is a formal way of saying 'reported' or 'informed.'
  • 如家人言 - Means 'as the family said,' indicating that what was observed matched what the family had reported.
  • 含忧悸不自安 - Means 'Han was worried and uneasy, unable to find peace,' where '忧悸' conveys a sense of anxiety and fear.
  • 至晓 - Means 'until dawn,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 金氏召含且令即市糜鹿 - Means 'Jin Shi called Han and ordered him to buy a milu deer immediately,' where '即市' means 'go to the market' and '糜鹿' refers to a type of deer.
  • 吾所须生者耳 - Means 'I only need the live one,' indicating that Jin Shi wanted a live animal.
  • 于是以生麋鹿致于前 - Means 'So, they brought a live milu deer to the front,' where '致' means 'to bring' or 'to present.'
  • 金氏啖立尽 - Means 'Jin Shi ate it all at once,' where '啖' means 'to eat' and '立尽' means 'all at once.'
  • 家人辈或窃语其事 - Means 'The family members sometimes whispered about the matter,' where '窃语' means 'whispered' or 'talked in secret.'
  • 色甚慙 - Means 'she looked very ashamed,' where '色' refers to appearance and '慙' means 'ashamed.'
  • 自是竟不还 - Means 'from then on, it never returned,' indicating that the wolf did not come back.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 能奈何憎人学问墨子兼爱其若是耶 - Rhetorical question expressing frustration or disbelief at someone's hatred of Mozi's doctrine of universal love; translated as 'How can one bear to hate the learning of Mozi, who advocates universal love, in this manner?'
  • 言卒便请退 - After finishing his words, he immediately requested to leave; '卒' indicates the completion of an action, and '便' suggests immediacy.
  • 华已使人防门不得出 - Hua had already sent someone to guard the door, preventing him from leaving; '防' means 'to guard' or 'to prevent.'
  • 既而又谓华曰 - Afterward, he again told Hua; '既而' indicates a sequence of events, and '谓' means 'to tell' or 'to say.'
  • 公门置甲兵栏骑当是疑于仆也 - In the public office, the placement of armed guards and mounted soldiers should be a sign of suspicion toward me; '置' means 'to place,' and '疑' means 'to suspect.'
  • 将恐天下之人卷舌而不言 - It will likely cause people to remain silent and not speak out; '卷舌' is a metaphor for silence.
  • 智谋之士望门而不进 - Wise and strategic individuals will hesitate to enter; '望门' means 'to look at the door' or 'to hesitate to enter.'
  • 深为明公惜之 - I deeply pity the distinguished gentleman for this; '惜' means 'to pity' or 'to regret.'
  • 华不应而使人御防甚严 - Hua did not respond but ordered even stricter guards to be posted; '御防' means 'to guard' or 'to protect.'
  • 丰城令雷焕博物士也 - The magistrate of Fengcheng, Lei Huan, is a learned scholar; '博物' means 'learned' or 'well-versed in many things.'
  • 闻魑魅忌狗所别者数百年物耳 - I have heard that ghosts and demons fear dogs, and they can only distinguish things that have been around for several hundred years; '别' means 'to distinguish.'
  • 千年老精不复能别 - A thousand-year-old spirit can no longer distinguish; '老精' refers to an ancient spirit.
  • 唯有千年枯木照之则形见 - Only a thousand-year-old withered tree, when illuminated, reveals its form; '形见' means 'to reveal its form.'
  • 燕昭王墓前华表已当千年乃遣人伐之 - The stone pillars in front of King Yan Zhao's tomb, which have been there for a thousand years, were then ordered to be cut down; '华表' refers to stone pillars.
  • 使人既至华表叹曰 - When the people arrived at the stone pillars, they sighed and said; '叹' means 'to sigh.'
  • 老狸自不自知果误我事于华表 - The old fox did not realize it, but it indeed caused me trouble at the stone pillars; '狸' refers to a fox.
  • 空中得青衣小儿长二尺余 - In the air, they found a small boy dressed in green, about two feet tall; '青衣' means 'green clothing.'
  • 将还至洛阳而变成枯木 - As they were returning to Luoyang, it transformed into a withered tree; '枯木' means 'withered tree.'
  • 燃之以照书生乃是一斑狸 - When it was lit, it revealed itself to be a spotted fox; '斑狸' means 'spotted fox.'
  • 茂先叹曰 - Maoxian sighed and said; '叹' means 'to sigh.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 墨子兼爱 - Refers to Mozi's doctrine of universal love, which advocates for impartial care for all people.
  • 华表 - Refers to stone pillars often placed in front of tombs or public buildings in ancient China, symbolizing officialdom or public notice.
  • 燕昭王 - Refers to King Yan Zhao, a historical figure known for his wisdom and the construction of the famous 'Yan's Tomb' in the state of Yan during the Warring States period.
  • 魑魅 - Refers to ghosts or demons in Chinese folklore, often associated with malevolent spirits.
  • 茂先 - Likely refers to Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and historian of the Jin dynasty, known for his work 'Shuowen Jiezi' (Explanations of Simple and Compound Characters).

Proper Nouns

  • 墨子 - Mozi, a philosopher and founder of the Mohist school.
  • 华 - Hua, likely a person of authority or influence.
  • 燕昭王 - King Yan Zhao, a historical figure.
  • 魑魅 - Ghosts or demons in Chinese folklore.
  • 茂先 - Likely Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and historian.
  • 丰城令雷焕 - The magistrate of Fengcheng, Lei Huan.

  • 君枉殺吾婦何诬得言狸 - "枉殺" means to kill unjustly, "何诬" means how can one falsely accuse, and "得言狸" means say it's a weasel, indicating the man accuses the protagonist of killing his wife unjustly.
  • 狸今何在 - Means "where is the weasel now," indicating the man asks where the weasel is.
  • 因共至坑視狸已成婦人 - "因共至坑視" means they went together to the pit to see, and "狸已成婦人" means the weasel has turned into a woman, indicating they see the weasel has transformed into a woman.
  • 男子因防孝子赴官應償死 - "防" means to prevent, "赴官" means to go to the official, and "應償死" means should be punished with death, indicating the man tries to prevent the protagonist from going to the official to face punishment.
  • 乃謂令曰此寔妖魅 - "乃謂令曰" means then he told the official, and "此寔妖魅" means this is indeed an evil spirit, indicating the man tells the official that it is an evil spirit.
  • 但出猎犬則可知魅 - "但出猎犬" means just bring out a hunting dog, and "則可知魅" means then it can be known, indicating the man suggests using a hunting dog to identify the evil spirit.
  • 復來催殺孝子 - Means the evil spirit comes again to urge the killing of the protagonist.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 絶无怪矣元忠歴大官至侍中中书令仆射则天崩中宗在谅闇诏元忠摄冡宰百官总已以听三日年八十余方薨 - This long sentence must be read as a sequence of events: there is no wonder that Yuan Zhong, who had held high office up to the positions of Chief Secretary, Grand Commandant, and Minister of Works, passed away three days after Wu Zetian's death, during the mourning period for Emperor Zhongzong, after assuming the role of prime minister for three days.
  • 始元忠微时常谒张景藏景藏待之甚薄就质通塞亦不答也乃大怒曰仆千里裹粮而来非徒然也必谓明公有以见敎而乃金口木舌以相遇殊不尽勤勤之意耶然富贵正由苍苍何预公事因拂衣长揖而去景藏遽牵止之曰君相正在怒中后当贵极人臣卒如其言 - This long sentence must be read as a sequence of events: when Yuan Zhong was not yet famous, he visited Zhang Jingcang, who treated him very coldly. When Yuan Zhong inquired about what was clear and what was obscure, Zhang Jingcang did not answer. Yuan Zhong became very angry and said, 'I have traveled a thousand miles with provisions, not for nothing. I thought you would have something to teach me, but you are inattentive and do not listen, which is not the way to show earnestness. However, fortune is in a state of anger, and you will later become the most powerful minister.' Zhang Jingcang immediately pulled him back and said, 'Your fortune is in a state of anger, and you will later become the most powerful minister.' This prediction came true.
  • 戸部尚书韦虚已其子常昼日独坐閤中忽闻檐际有声顾视乃牛头人眞地狱图中所见者据其所下窥之韦伏不敢动须臾登阶诣牀前面临其上如此者三乃下去韦子不胜其惧复将出内即以枕掷之不中乃开其门趋前逐之韦子呌呼但绕一空井而走迫之转急遂投于井中其物因据井而坐韦仰观之乃变为一猿良久家人至猿即不见视井旁有足迹奔蹂之状怪之窥井中乃见韦子在焉悬缒出之恍惚不能言三日方能说月余乃卒 - This long sentence must be read as a sequence of events: the son of the Minister of Finance, Wei Xuyi, was sitting alone in his office during the day when he suddenly heard a sound from the eaves. He looked up and saw a man with a bull's head, just like the one in a hell picture, who descended and peered at him. Wei Xuyi crouched down and did not dare to move. After a while, the man climbed the stairs and approached the bed, facing him from above. This happened three times before he left. Wei Xuyi was so terrified that he tried to escape, but he threw a pillow at the man, who did not hit him. The man then opened the door and chased him. Wei Xuyi cried out but only ran around an empty well. As he was being chased more urgently, he fell into the well. The man then sat on the well, and Wei Xuyi looked up and saw the man turn into a monkey. After a long time, his family arrived, and the monkey was no longer there. They saw footprints around the well and were surprised. They looked into the well and saw Wei Xuyi inside. They pulled him out with a rope, and he was in a daze and could not speak. After three days, he could speak, but he died after a month.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其便恪遂携剑隐于室内而终有难色 - Describes a situation where the person named '恪' (Ke) hides his sword in the room and shows signs of difficulty or hesitation.
  • 袁氏俄觉大怒而责恪曰 - "俄觉" means 'suddenly felt,' and "责" means 'to rebuke' or 'to scold.' This phrase indicates that the person named '袁氏' (Yuan) suddenly became angry and scolded Ke.
  • 子之穷愁我使畅泰不顾恩义遂兴非为如此用心则犬不食其余岂能立节行于人世也 - A complex sentence where "子之穷愁我使畅泰" means 'your poverty and distress I have made you prosperous,' and "不顾恩义遂兴非为" means 'you disregard gratitude and righteousness and engage in improper actions.' The phrase "如此用心则犬不食其余" is a metaphor suggesting that if one acts in such a way, even dogs would not eat the rest, implying extreme disapproval. "岂能立节行于人世也" means 'how can one maintain integrity and live in the world?'
  • 恪既被责惭顔惕虑叩头曰受教于表兄非宿心也愿以饮血为盟更不敢有他意汗落伏地 - "恪既被责惭顔惕虑" means 'Ke, after being scolded, felt ashamed and fearful,' and "叩头曰" means 'knelt down and said.' "受教于表兄非宿心也" means 'I was taught by my cousin, not out of my own will.' "愿以饮血为盟更不敢有他意" means 'I wish to swear an oath with blood and will not have any other intentions.' "汗落伏地" means 'sweat fell to the ground.'
  • 袁氏遂搜得其剑寸折之若防轻藕耳恪愈惧似欲奔迸袁氏乃笑曰张生一小子不能以道义诲其表弟使行其凶险来当辱之然观子之心的应不如是然吾匹君已数岁矣子何虑哉 - "袁氏遂搜得其剑寸折之" means 'Yuan then searched and found the sword, breaking it into pieces.' "若防轻藕耳" is a metaphor suggesting that the sword was treated as if it were a light reed. "恪愈惧似欲奔迸" means 'Ke became even more afraid and seemed to want to flee.' "张生一小子不能以道义诲其表弟使行其凶险来当辱之" means 'Zhang, a young man, could not teach his cousin-in-law righteousness, leading him to commit dangerous acts, which is shameful.' "然观子之心的应不如是然吾匹君已数岁矣子何虑哉" means 'However, looking at your heart, it should not be like that. We have been together for several years; why are you worried?'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '其便恪遂携剑隐于室内而终有难色' is elliptical and requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The sentence '子之穷愁我使畅泰不顾恩义遂兴非为如此用心则犬不食其余岂能立节行于人世也' is a complex sentence with multiple clauses, each conveying a different aspect of the rebuke.
  • The sentence '恪既被责惭顔惕虑叩头曰受教于表兄非宿心也愿以饮血为盟更不敢有他意汗落伏地' is also complex, with a series of actions and statements that need to be clearly separated.
  • The long sentence '袁氏遂搜得其剑寸折之若防轻藕耳恪愈惧似欲奔迸袁氏乃笑曰张生一小子不能以道义诲其表弟使行其凶险来当辱之然观子之心的应不如是然吾匹君已数岁矣子何虑哉' contains multiple actions and dialogues, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.

  • 伯裘 - The name of the servant.
  • 李音 - The name of the secretary.
  • 诸侯 - The names of the feudal lords are not specified.

[5251]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 取妻三年妻不脱衣而卧 - Narrative device suggesting unusual or supernatural behavior, not a literal description of a sleep habit.
  • 阴解其衣 - Secretly unfastened her clothes, implying stealth and possibly a supernatural act.
  • 变为一狐 - Became a fox, a common motif in Chinese folklore for supernatural beings.
  • 防衣 - Possibly a variant of fangyi, which could be a type of protective clothing or a specific term for a traditional garment. This term is ambiguous and may be better left as fangyi with a note.
  • 拉然而崩 - Collapsed suddenly, a dramatic expression used to describe a sudden, violent collapse.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 后魏 - Refers to the Northern Wei, one of the northern dynasties of the Northern and Southern Dynasties period. Rendered as 'the Northern Wei dynasty'.
  • 挽歌者 - A mourner or a person who sings funeral songs, likely referring to someone who sings at funerals, possibly a professional mourner or a person associated with death rituals.
  • 淳于智 - A person’s name, possibly a diviner or shaman. Rendered as Chunyu Zhi.
  • 捜神记 - Correct title is 《搜神记》 (Soug Shen Ji), a famous anthology of supernatural stories compiled by Gan Bao during the Eastern Jin dynasty. Rendered as 'Records of the Supernatural' or 'Soug Shen Ji' with an explanatory note.
  • 京邑 - Refers to the capital city, likely Luoyang during the Northern Wei period. Rendered as 'the capital city' unless context allows for a specific name.

Proper Nouns

  • 孙岩 - A person’s name. Rendered as Sun Yan.
  • 夏侯藻 - A person’s name. Rendered as Xiahou Zao.
  • 淳于智 - A person’s name. Rendered as Chunyu Zhi.
